title: Newton's Third Law
subtitle: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: You push a wall with 50 N of force. What force does the wall push back on you?
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Students often think the wall pushes back less (or not at all) because it doesn’t move. Newton’s Third Law: the wall pushes back with EXACTLY 50 N. The forces are equal and opposite, but act on DIFFERENT objects.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Exactly right. The wall pushes back with 50 N. N3L pairs are always equal, opposite, same type, and act on different objects.

steps.title: Newton’s Third Law
steps.bodyHTML: When object A pushes on object B, object B pushes back on A with an equal force in the opposite direction. These are called Newton’s Third Law pairs. They are always the same type of force, same size, opposite direction, and act on different objects. They never cancel out because they act on different bodies.
commonErrors: Thinking N3L pairs cancel out (they act on DIFFERENT objects)
Confusing N3L pairs with balanced forces
Thinking the reaction force is smaller when an object doesn’t move

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change force and mass. Watch how acceleration responds in real time.

aqa.questionText: A swimmer pushes backward on the water with her feet. Use Newton’s Third Law to explain how she moves forward.

steps.label: Identify the action force
steps.content: The swimmer pushes backward on the water with her feet.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — identifies the action force on the water

steps.label: Apply Newton’s Third Law
steps.content: By Newton’s Third Law, the water pushes back on the swimmer with an equal force in the opposite (forward) direction.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — states equal and opposite force on the swimmer

steps.label: Explain the result
steps.content: This forward force from the water on the swimmer causes her to accelerate forward.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — links the reaction force to forward motion

aqa.finalAnswer: The water exerts an equal and opposite forward force on the swimmer, causing her to move forward.

edexcel.questionText: A person stands on the ground. Identify the Newton’s Third Law pair for the person’s weight.

steps.label: Identify the person’s weight
steps.content: The person’s weight is the gravitational pull of the Earth on the person (downward).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — identifies weight as Earth pulling person

steps.label: State the N3L pair
steps.content: The N3L pair is the gravitational pull of the person on the Earth (upward).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct pair identified acting on the Earth

steps.label: Confirm the pair properties
steps.content: Both forces are gravitational, equal in size, opposite in direction, and act on different objects (person and Earth).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — states same type, equal size, different objects

edexcel.finalAnswer: The N3L pair is the gravitational pull of the person on the Earth — equal in size, opposite in direction, same type of force, acting on a different object.

ocr-gateway.source: OCR Gateway style · Newton’s Third Law
ocr-gateway.questionText: A rocket expels hot gas downward. Use Newton’s Third Law to explain why it moves upward.

steps.label: Identify the action force
steps.content: The rocket pushes hot gas downward with a large force.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — identifies the force on the gas

steps.label: Apply N3L
steps.content: By Newton’s Third Law, the gas pushes back on the rocket with an equal force in the upward direction, causing the rocket to accelerate upward.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — equal and opposite force on rocket causes upward motion

ocr-gateway.finalAnswer: The gas exerts an equal and opposite upward force on the rocket, propelling it upward.

ocr-21c.source: OCR 21st Century style · Newton’s Third Law
ocr-21c.questionText: Two ice skaters push against each other. Skater A (60 kg) and Skater B (80 kg). If A pushes with 120 N, what force acts on A from B? Who accelerates more and why?

steps.label: Apply N3L for the force
steps.content: By Newton’s Third Law, skater B pushes back on skater A with exactly 120 N (equal and opposite).
steps.equation: F on A from B = 120 N
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — 120 N by Newton’s Third Law

steps.label: Calculate accelerations
steps.content: Using F = ma for each skater:

steps.label: State who accelerates more
steps.content: Skater A accelerates more because they have a smaller mass. The forces are equal (N3L), but the lighter skater gets a larger acceleration (N2L: a = F/m).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct reasoning linking N3L and N2L

ocr-21c.finalAnswer: 120 N acts on A. Skater A accelerates more (2.0 m/s² vs 1.5 m/s²) because same force but smaller mass.

wjec.questionText: A ball hits a bat. The ball exerts 500 N on the bat. State the force the bat exerts on the ball.

steps.label: Apply N3L
steps.content: By Newton’s Third Law, the bat exerts a force of 500 N on the ball.

steps.label: State direction
steps.content: The force acts in the opposite direction to the force the ball exerts on the bat.

aqa.questionText: A book sits on a table. The book pushes down on the table with 15 N. What is the force the table exerts on the book?

options.label: A
options.text: 0 N — the table is not alive so cannot push

options.label: B
options.text: 15 N upward

options.label: C
options.text: Less than 15 N upward

options.label: D
options.text: 15 N downward

markSchemeSteps: By N3L the table pushes back on the book
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark correct size · 1 mark correct direction
aqa.commonMistake: Option D is a trap — the reaction force must be in the OPPOSITE direction.

edexcel.questionText: A horse pulls a cart forward with 800 N. By Newton’s Third Law, what force does the cart exert on the horse?

options.label: A
options.text: 800 N backward

options.label: B
options.text: 800 N forward

options.label: C
options.text: 0 N — the cart cannot pull

options.label: D
options.text: Less than 800 N because the cart is heavier

markSchemeSteps: N3L: cart pulls back on horse with equal force
edexcel.markSchemeAward: 1 mark correct size · 1 mark correct direction
edexcel.commonMistake: Option D reflects the misconception that heavier objects exert different N3L forces — they do not.

ocr-gateway.questionText: An astronaut in space throws a tool away from them. What happens to the astronaut and why?

options.label: A
options.text: The astronaut moves in the opposite direction — the tool pushes back on them (N3L)

options.label: B
options.text: The astronaut stays still — there is no gravity in space

options.label: C
options.text: The astronaut moves in the same direction as the tool

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing happens — N3L only works on Earth

markSchemeSteps: By N3L, the tool exerts an equal and opposite force on the astronaut
The astronaut moves in the opposite direction to the tool ✓
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark N3L stated · 1 mark correct direction of motion
ocr-gateway.commonMistake: Option B wrongly links weightlessness with no forces — contact forces still produce N3L pairs in space.

ocr-21c.questionText: Two magnets repel each other. Magnet A (mass 0.2 kg) and Magnet B (mass 0.5 kg) each experience a force of 4 N. Which magnet accelerates more?

options.label: A
options.text: Magnet A — smaller mass so larger acceleration (a = F/m = 20 m/s²)

options.label: B
options.text: Magnet B — larger mass means larger acceleration

options.label: C
options.text: Both accelerate equally — same force means same acceleration

options.label: D
options.text: Neither accelerates — the forces cancel out

markSchemeSteps: N3L: both experience 4 N (equal and opposite)
Magnet A accelerates more — smaller mass ✓
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark N3L forces equal · 1 mark correct accelerations · 1 mark correct reasoning
ocr-21c.commonMistake: Option D is a common error — the forces act on DIFFERENT objects so they do not cancel.

wjec.questionText: A car tyre pushes backward on the road with 3000 N. What force does the road exert on the tyre?

options.label: A
options.text: 3000 N forward

options.label: B
options.text: 3000 N backward

options.label: C
options.text: 0 N — the road is stationary

options.label: D
options.text: More than 3000 N because the road is heavier

markSchemeSteps: N3L: road pushes on tyre with equal and opposite force
wjec.markSchemeAward: 1 mark correct size · 1 mark correct direction
wjec.commonMistake: Option C wrongly assumes stationary objects cannot exert forces.

rearrangements.label: Find force of A on B
Find force of B on A

equation.examTip: Always state: equal in size, opposite in direction, same type, act on DIFFERENT objects. That’s 4 marks in most boards.

boardNotes.aqa: Not on the formula sheet. State all four properties of N3L pairs for full marks: equal size, opposite direction, same type, different objects.
boardNotes.edexcel: Not on the equation sheet. Edexcel often asks you to ‘identify the interaction pair’ — name both forces and both objects.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: Not on the formula sheet. OCR Gateway frequently tests N3L in context of rockets and propulsion.
boardNotes.ocr-21c: Not on the formula sheet. OCR 21C likes combining N3L with N2L — expect to calculate accelerations of both objects.
boardNotes.wjec: Not on the formula sheet. WJEC questions often ask you to ‘state’ the reaction force — always include size AND direction.
